title: BCS-BEC crossover in trapped one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard chains: entanglement and correlation signatures from DMRG and effective-pairing theory
authors: G. Diniz, I. M. Carvalho, M. Sanino, F. Iemini, V. V. França
abstract: Confined ultracold atoms in optical lattices provide a versatile platform for simulating lattice models of strongly correlated quantum systems, where pairing phenomena and superfluid phases can be explored under controlled conditions. While the crossover between the Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) phase and the Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) is well understood in homogeneous systems, spatial confinement breaks translational symmetry and reshapes correlation patterns, making the BCS-BEC identification in trapped geometries challenging and allowing unconventional phases to emerge with no direct analog in homogeneous systems. Here we present a characterization of the BCS-BEC crossover in harmonically confined one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard chains. Our analysis combines Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) simulations and entanglement-based diagnostics with effective models describing the formation of tightly bound fermion pairs. This combined approach enables a detailed understanding of how the interplay between interactions and confinement reshapes the crossover, leading to insulating regions coexisting with persistent superfluid correlations. Within this framework, we further introduce conditioned correlation functions whose power-law decay allows a clear distinction between BCS-like and BEC-like regimes. The consistency between the effective descriptions and the numerical DMRG results yields a unified picture of the crossover in harmonically confined geometries.
summary: This paper investigates how harmonic confinement reshapes the BCS-BEC crossover in 1D Fermi-Hubbard chains. By using DMRG and effective models, the authors show that confinement creates a unique phase structure where insulating and superfluid regions coexist. They introduce new correlation-based tools to clearly identify the transition between weakly bound Cooper pairs and tightly bound molecules.
main_problem: Characterizing the BCS-BEC crossover and the superfluid-to-insulator transition in one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard chains subject to harmonic confinement.
main_result: The study identifies distinct power-law scaling regimes for pair sizes in the BCS and BEC limits and demonstrates that confinement leads to a composite phase where insulating regions coexist with superfluid edges.
method: The authors combine Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) simulations with effective-pairing theory, including mean-field analysis and second-order perturbation theory.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model with attractive on-site interactions (U < 0) subject to a quadratic/harmonic confining potential.
key_observables: Half-chain entanglement entropy, four-particle correlation functions, root-mean's distance of pair distribution, density profiles, and Bogoliubov quasiparticle spectra.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (U/t), confinement strength (k), particle density (n), and chain length (L).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the effective-pairing theory in the strong-coupling limit and uses the local-density approximation as a point of comparison.
figures_summary: Figures include phase diagrams of the crossover, energy spectra of the confined model, density profiles comparing DMRG and effective models, and entanglement entropy plots identifying phase transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of confinement-induced symmetry breaking, presents numerical DMRG results, develops effective models for the BEC and BCS limits, analyzes entanglement and correlation-based diagnostics, and concludes with a unified phase diagram.
why_interesting: It provides robust entanglement-based diagnostics to distinguish pairing regimes in inhomogeneous systems, which is highly relevant for interpreting experiments with ultracold atoms in optical lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Phase diagram predicted by the effective models as function of the interaction strength |U| and average density n. Solid lines indicate the boundaries between BCS- and BEC- dominated regimes (black), using the criterion rpair ≤1 from Eq. (10); and the insulator (INS) and the superfluid (SF) phase (red), adopting the criterion maxj(nj) ≥1.99. For comparison we also include DMRG results: × symbols (entanglement-based method) and square symbols (correlator-based method). The red dashed line represents Eq. (6), showing good agreement for |U| ≳6t; while the blue dashed line indicates the interaction value |U| = 4t, above which no BCS regime is observed. All data are for fixed k =...

Figure 2. Schematic representation of the effective models derived from the confined Fermi-Hubbard model in Eq. (1). Strongly coupled limit: For |U| ≫t, perturbation theory yields an effective description of the BEC regime (Eq. (2)) with confinement strength 2k, hopping τ = 2t2/|U|, and nearest-neighbor repulsion 2τ. Hard-core bosonic tight-binding: When the density varies smoothly, this model further reduces to a confined tight-binding chain with hopping 2τ (Eq. (5)). Through the Girardeau mapping [53], together with the renormalized confinement strength ˜k = |U|k/2t, the system can equivalently be described as a confined spinless fermionic tight-binding chain. For |U| ≪t and finite system...

Figure 3. Spectrum ϵl of the confined tight-binding model with k = 0.002 and L = 100, where l indexes eigenstates by increasing energy. The blue dashed line shows a harmonic fit, while the black dashed lines mark the boundary between harmonic and localized states. Notice that the localized states are degenerate due to the system’s even symmetry.

Figure 6. Mean-field quasiparticle spectrum for U = −0.25t (black) and U = −2t (red), with fixed n = 1 and k = 0.002. The dashed lines delimitates the region where pairing is concentrated for U = −2t. The left side shows harmonic-like states, while the right contains localized states, as also seen in the confined tight- binding chain in Fig. 3.
